The equation
\[
S(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} f_n(x) = a
\]
holds for all \(x \in [0,1]\), where \(f_0(x) = x\) and \(f_n = f^{\circ n}\) (i.e., \(f\) composed with itself \(n\) times) for \(n \geq 1\). The function \(f : [0,1] \to [0,1]\) is continuous.

Since \(S(f(x)) = a\) as well,
\[
S(f(x)) = \sum_{n=1}^{k} f_n(x) = S(x) - x + f_k(x) = a - x + f^k(x).
\]
Thus \(a - x + f^k(x) = a\), so
\[
f^k(x) = x
\]
for all \(x \in [0,1]\). In particular, \(f\) is bijective (with inverse \(f^{k-1}\)) and hence a homeomorphism of \([0,1]\) onto itself. Any homeomorphism of \([0,1]\) is strictly monotonic (either increasing or decreasing).

First suppose \(f\) is strictly increasing. Then \(f(0) = 0\) and \(f(1) = 1\). Suppose there exists \(x_0 \in (0,1)\) with \(f(x_0) > x_0\). Iterating and using that each \(f^j\) (\(j \geq 1\)) is strictly increasing yields
\[
f(x_0) < f^2(x_0) < \cdots < f^k(x_0),
\]
so \(f^k(x_0) > x_0\), contradicting \(f^k(x_0) = x_0\). Similarly, \(f(x_0) < x_0\) implies \(f^k(x_0) < x_0\), again a contradiction. Thus \(f(x) = x\) for all \(x \in [0,1]\) (the identity). But then \(S(x) = kx = a\) for all \(x \in [0,1]\), which is impossible (as the right-hand side is constant while the left-hand side is not).

\[
S(x) = m \cdot x + m \cdot f(x) = m(x + f(x)).

\[

Since \(\{e_k\}_{k=1}^{2026}\) is any orthonormal set of size \(M=2026\), the sum is at most the sum of the \(M\) largest eigenvalues of \(A\) (by the variational characterization of eigenvalues for compact self-adjoint operators; equivalently, it is the trace of \(P_V A P_V\) maximized over \(M\)-dimensional subspaces \(V\), which is achieved when \(V\) is spanned by the top eigenspaces).

### Spectral analysis of \(A\).
To compute the eigenvalues of \(A\), apply \(A\) to a candidate eigenfunction \(f\) with \(Af = \lambda f\) (for \(\lambda > 0\)) and differentiate twice (justified by absolute continuity of the antiderivative, since \(f \in L^2[0,1] \subset L^1[0,1]\)): this yields
\[
g(x) := (Af)(x), \qquad g'(x) = -\int_0^x f(y)\,dy, \qquad g''(x) = -f(x).
\]
The boundary conditions are immediate: \(g(1) = 0\) (directly from the kernel), and \(g'(0) = 0\). Substituting \(g = \lambda f\) into \(g'' = -f\) produces the eigenvalue problem
\[
f'' + \frac1\lambda f = 0, \qquad f'(0) = 0, \qquad f(1) = 0.
\]
(The boundary conditions translate directly to \(f\), since \(\lambda > 0\).) The general solution is \(f(x) = \cos(\sqrt{\mu}\, x)\) with \(\mu = 1/\lambda\) (the sine term is ruled out by \(f'(0)=0\)). The condition \(f(1)=0\) forces \(\sqrt{\mu} = (2j+1)\pi/2\) for \(j = 0,1,2,\dots\), or equivalently
\[
\mu_j = \Bigl( \frac{(2j+1)\pi}2 \Bigr)^2, \qquad \lambda_j = \frac4{((2j+1)\pi)^2}, \qquad j=0,1,2,\dots
\]
(with eigenfunctions \(\cos(((2j+1)\pi x)/2)\)). These are indeed all the eigenvalues (the problem is a standard regular Sturm--Liouville eigenvalue problem on \([0,1]\) with these separated boundary conditions; the eigenfunctions form a complete orthogonal basis). Note that they are decreasing in \(j\), and their sum is
\[
\sum_{j=0}^\infty \lambda_j = \frac4{\pi^2} \sum_{j=0}^\infty \frac1{(2j+1)^2} = \frac4{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}8 = \frac12,
\]
where the sum over odds is \(\sum 1/k^2 - \sum 1/(2l)^2 = \pi^2/6 - (\pi^2/24) = \pi^2/8\) (as is standard from Parseval on the Fourier series of \(x^2\) or direct computation). This is consistent, since taking the full basis would yield \(s(t) = t\) and \(\int_0^1 s(t)\,dt = 1/2\).

### Tail estimate and contradiction.
In our case,
\[
\int_0^1 s(t)\,dt \leq \sum_{j=0}^{2025} \lambda_j = \frac12 - \sum_{j=2026}^\infty \lambda_j = \frac12 - \frac4{\pi^2} \sum_{j=2026}^\infty \frac1{(2j+1)^2}.
\]
For the tail, use that \(x \mapsto 1/(2x+1)^2\) is positive and decreasing for \(x \geq 0\), so
\[
\sum_{j=2026}^\infty \frac1{(2j+1)^2} > \int_{2026}^\infty \frac{dx}{(2x+1)^2} = \Bigl[ -\frac1{2(2x+1)} \Bigr]_{2026}^\infty = \frac1{2(2\cdot2026 + 1)} = \frac1{2 \cdot 4053}.
\]
Thus the remainder is
\[
\sum_{j=2026}^\infty \lambda_j > \frac4{\pi^2} \cdot \frac1{2\cdot4053} = \frac2{\pi^2 \cdot 4053}.
\]
It remains to check that this exceeds \(\delta = 1/(10000 \times 2026)\), i.e., that
\[
\frac1{10000 \times 2026} < \frac2{\pi^2 \cdot 4053} \iff \pi^2 \cdot 4053 < 2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 2026 = 40520000.
\]
The left side satisfies \(\pi^2 \cdot 4053 < 10 \cdot 4053 = 40530 < 40520000\), as required. (More sharply, \(\pi^2 < 9.87\) gives a left side \(< 40003\), but the cruder bound suffices.) Therefore \(\delta < \sum_{j=2026}^\infty \lambda_j\), which forces
\[
\int_0^1 s(t)\,dt \geq \frac12 - \delta > \frac12 - \sum_{j=2026}^\infty \lambda_j \geq \sum_{j=0}^{2025} \lambda_j,
\]
contradicting the upper bound on the integral in terms of the top eigenvalues. This completes the contradiction. Hence there must exist some \(k > 2026\) and some \(t \in [0,1]\) such that
\[
\Bigl| \int_0^t e_k(x)\,dx \Bigr| > \frac1{100k}.
\]
(Note: the argument is insensitive to whether the Hilbert space is real or complex, since the operator \(A\) is the same and the estimates on \(\langle A e,e \rangle\) depend only on the real eigenvalues.) 

(Note also that the specific constants 2026 and 100 are chosen precisely so that the tail estimate on the \(\lambda_j\) exceeds \(\delta\); any smaller constants on the right-hand side of the desired inequality, or any smaller lower threshold on \(k\), would work equally well, but the given ones suffice.)

### Setup and contradiction assumption.
We argue by contradiction. Suppose that
\[
  \biggl| \int_0^t e_k(x)\,dx \biggr| \le \frac1{100k}
\]
holds for _all_ \(k>2026\) and _all_ \(t\in[0,1]\). Let \(V\) denote the (closed) span of \(\{e_1,\dots,e_{2026}\}\), so that \(\dim V\le 2026\). (In fact equality holds by orthonormality, but we only need the upper bound on dimension.)

Let \(m=2027\), and define points \(t_j=j/m\) for \(j=0,\dots,m\). Define the indicator functions
\[
  \psi_j := \mathbf{1}_{[t_{j-1},t_j]} \qquad (j=1,\dots,m).
\]
These are pairwise orthogonal in \(L^2[0,1]\) (by disjoint supports), with \(\|\psi_j\|^2=1/m>0\), so the span \(W:=\mathrm{span}\{\psi_1,\dots,\psi_m\}\) is an \(m\)-dimensional subspace of \(L^2[0,1]\) (with \(\dim W=m=2027\)).

By a standard dimension count,
\[
  \dim(W\cap V^\perp) = \dim W - \dim(W\cap V) \ge \dim W - \dim V \ge 2027-2026=1,
\]
where we used \(\dim(W\cap V)\le\dim V\) (since \(W\cap V\subset V\)). Thus there exists \(f\in W\cap V^\perp\) with \(\|f\|=1\). (Explicitly: take any nonzero vector in the intersection and normalize.)

### Application of Parseval.
Since \(\{e_k\}_{k\ge1}\) is an orthonormal basis of \(L^2[0,1]\), Parseval's identity gives
\[
  \|f\|^2 = \sum_{k=1}^\infty | \langle f,e_k\rangle |^2.
\]
But \(f\in V^\perp\) means \(\langle f,e_k\rangle=0\) for all \(k=1,\dots,2026\), so in fact
\[
  1 = \|f\|^2 = \sum_{k=2027}^\infty | \langle f,e_k\rangle |^2.
\]
We will derive an upper bound on the right-hand side strictly less than \(1\), yielding the desired contradiction.

### Estimation of the tail.
Since \(f\in W\), we may write \(f=\sum_{j=1}^m c_j \psi_j\) for coefficients \(c_j\in\mathbb{C}\) (the argument is identical for real or complex bases). Then
\[
  \|f\|^2 = \sum_{j=1}^m |c_j|^2 \cdot \frac1m =1 \implies \sum_{j=1}^m |c_j|^2 = m.
\]
For each \(k\ge2027\), we have
\[
  \langle f,e_k\rangle = \sum_{j=1}^m c_j \langle \psi_j,e_k\rangle = \sum_{j=1}^m c_j \Bigl( \int_0^{t_j} e_k(x)\,dx - \int_0^{t_{j-1}} e_k(x)\,dx \Bigr),
\]
so (writing \(F_k(t):=\int_0^t e_k(x)\,dx\))
\[
  | \langle f,e_k\rangle | \le \sum_{j=1}^m |c_j| \cdot |F_k(t_j)-F_k(t_{j-1})|.
\]
By the contradiction assumption, \(|F_k(\cdot)|\le 1/(100k)\) pointwise, so each difference is at most \(2/(100k)\). Thus
\[
  | \langle f,e_k\rangle | \le \frac2{100k} \sum_{j=1}^m |c_j|.
\]
By Cauchy--Schwarz,
\[
  \sum_{j=1}^m |c_j| \le \sqrt{m} \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^m |c_j|^2} = \sqrt{m}\cdot\sqrt{m} = m,
\]
and therefore
\[
  | \langle f,e_k\rangle | \le \frac{2m}{100k} = \frac{m}{50k}.
\]
Squaring and summing the tail,
\[
  \sum_{k=2027}^\infty | \langle f,e_k\rangle |^2 \le \sum_{k=2027}^\infty \frac{m^2}{2500 k^2} = \frac{m^2}{2500} \sum_{k=2027}^\infty \frac1{k^2}.
\]
For the remaining sum we use the integral test: since \(x\mapsto 1/x^2\) is positive and decreasing,
\[
  \sum_{k=2027}^\infty \frac1{k^2} < \int_{2026}^\infty \frac{dx}{x^2} = \frac1{2026}.
\]
Thus
\[
  1 = \sum_{k=2027}^\infty | \langle f,e_k\rangle |^2 < \frac{m^2}{2500} \cdot \frac1{2026} = \frac{2027^2}{2500\cdot 2026}.
\]
But now expand \(2027=2026+1\):
\[
  2027^2 = (2026+1)^2 = 2026^2 + 2\cdot2026 +1,
\]
so
\[
  \frac{2027^2}{2500\cdot2026} = \frac{2026}{2500} + \frac{2}{2500} + \frac{1}{2500\cdot2026} < \frac{2026}{2500} + \frac{2}{2500} + \frac1{2500\cdot2026}.
\]
Since \(2026/2500<0.8104\), \(2/2500=0.0008\), and \(1/(2500\cdot2026)<0.0000002\), the total is strictly less than \(0.8112<1\). This is the desired contradiction (\(1<1\)).

Thus the assumption is false: there must exist some \(k>2026\) and some \(t\in[0,1]\) such that
\[
  \biggl| \int_0^t e_k(x)\,dx \biggr| > \frac1{100k}.
\]
(Note: the argument is unaffected if \(\dim V<2026\), since this would only make \(\dim(W\cap V^\perp)\) even larger.)  
(Note also that the specific constants \(2026\) and \(100\) are immaterial, so long as \(m/(50\sqrt{2500\cdot(n)})>1\) for \(n=2026\) and \(m=n+1\); the argument works equally for any fixed \(N\) in place of \(2026\) and any fixed \(C>0\) in place of \(100\), with \(m=N+1\) and the bound \(2/C\) in place of \(2/100\).)

### Setup, assumption, and Parseval.

Let \(F_k(t) := \int_0^t e_k(x)\,dx\), so that \(F_k(t) = \langle \chi_{[0,t]}, e_k \rangle_{L^2[0,1]}\). Since \(\{e_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) is an orthonormal basis of \(L^2[0,1]\), Parseval's identity gives
\[
\sum_{k=1}^\infty |F_k(t)|^2 = \|\chi_{[0,t]}\|_{L^2[0,1]}^2 = t
\]
for each \(t \in [0,1]\) (with both sides continuous in \(t\)). Integrating over \([0,1]\) and applying Tonelli's theorem (all terms nonnegative) thus yields
\[
\sum_{k=1}^\infty \int_0^1 F_k(t)^2\,dt = \int_0^1 t\,dt = \frac12.
\]
In particular, writing \(I := \sum_{k=1}^N \int_0^1 F_k(t)^2\,dt\) for \(N=2026\), we have
\[
I = \frac12 - \sum_{k=N+1}^\infty \int_0^1 F_k(t)^2\,dt.
\]

Assume for contradiction that \(|F_k(t)| \le \frac1{100k}\) for all \(t \in [0,1]\) and all \(k > 2026\) (i.e., \(k \ge 2027\)). Then for each such \(k\),
\[
\int_0^1 F_k(t)^2\,dt \le \int_0^1 \Bigl(\frac1{100k}\Bigr)^2\,dt = \frac1{10000 k^2},
\]
and thus (since the sum is strictly smaller than the integral test tail)
\[
\sum_{k=2027}^\infty \int_0^1 F_k(t)^2\,dt < \frac1{10000} \sum_{k=2027}^\infty \frac1{k^2} < \frac1{10000} \int_{2026}^\infty \frac{dx}{x^2} = \frac1{10000 \times 2026}.
\]
It follows that
\[
I > \frac12 - \frac1{10000 \times 2026}.
\]

### The associated integral operator and its spectral decomposition.

On the other hand, we express each term \(\int_0^1 F_k(t)^2\,dt\) variationally. Expanding the quadratic form gives
\[
\int_0^1 F_k(t)^2\,dt = \iint_{[0,1]^2} e_k(x) e_k(y) (1 - \max(x,y))\,dx\,dy = \langle K e_k, e_k \rangle_{L^2[0,1]},
\]
where \(K\) is the compact self-adjoint positive-semidefinite integral operator on \(L^2[0,1]\) with continuous kernel \(K(x,y) = 1 - \max(x,y)\). (Explicitly, \(Kf(x) = (1-x)\int_0^x f(y)\,dy + \int_x^1 (1-y)f(y)\,dy\).) Thus \(I = \sum_{k=1}^N \langle K e_k, e_k \rangle\).

To diagonalize \(K\), we solve the eigenvalue problem \(Kg = \lambda g\) (for \(\lambda > 0\)). Differentiating the integral equation twice (justified _a posteriori_ since the kernel is continuous, so eigenfunctions are \(C^1\) and the image of \(K\) lies in \(C^2\)) yields \(g'' + \frac1\lambda g = 0\), or \(g(x) = C \cos(\omega x) + D \sin(\omega x)\) with \(\omega^2 = 1/\lambda\). The boundary conditions are obtained by evaluating the differentiated forms: first, \(L(x) := Kg(x)\) satisfies \(L'(x) = -\int_0^x g(y)\,dy\), so \(\lambda g'(x) = L'(x)\) implies \(g'(0) = 0\); second, \(L(1) = 0\) implies \(g(1) = 0\). Thus \(D = 0\) and \(\cos(\omega) = 0\), so \(\omega = (2m+1)\pi/2\) for \(m = 0,1,2,\dots\), with eigenvalues
\[
\lambda_m = \frac4{\pi^2 (2m+1)^2}, \qquad m=0,1,2,\dots,
\]
and eigenfunctions \(\cos(((2m+1)\pi x)/2)\) (which indeed form a complete orthogonal basis by the standard Sturm--Liouville theory for the equivalent boundary-value problem \(-u'' = \mu u\), \(u'(0)=u(1)=0\)). (This exhausts the spectrum: the derivation shows any eigenfunction must satisfy the ODE and boundary conditions.) In particular, \(\sum_{m=0}^\infty \lambda_m = \frac4{\pi^2} \sum_{m=0}^\infty \frac1{(2m+1)^2} = \frac4{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}8 = \frac12\), as expected from the trace formula \(\operatorname{tr}(K) = \int_0^1 (1-x)\,dx = 1/2\).

By the minimax principle, for _any_ choice of \(N\) orthonormal vectors (in particular, our \(\{e_1,\dots,e_N\}\)),
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \langle K e_k, e_k \rangle \le \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \lambda_m = \frac4{\pi^2} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \frac1{(2m+1)^2} = \frac12 - \frac4{\pi^2} \sum_{m=N}^\infty \frac1{(2m+1)^2}.
\]
Thus \(I \le \frac12 - \frac4{\pi^2} \sum_{m=2026}^\infty \frac1{(2m+1)^2}\). To obtain the desired contradiction, it suffices to show that the tail satisfies
\[
\frac4{\pi^2} \sum_{m=2026}^\infty \frac1{(2m+1)^2} > \frac1{10000 \times 2026}.
\]
Since \(\pi^2 < 10\), we have \(\frac4{\pi^2} > 0.4 = 2/5\). For the sum, let \(f(x) = 1/(2x+1)^2\) (positive and decreasing for \(x \ge 0\)); the integral test gives
\[
\sum_{m=2026}^\infty f(m) > \int_{2026}^\infty f(x)\,dx = \int_{2026}^\infty \frac{dx}{(2x+1)^2} = \Bigl[ -\frac1{2(2x+1)} \Bigr]_{2026}^\infty = \frac1{2(2\cdot2026 +1)} = \frac1{8106}.
\]
(Here the inequality \(\sum_{m=n}^\infty f(m) > \int_n^\infty f(x)\,dx\) follows by comparing \(f(n) > \int_n^{n+1} f\) and \(\sum_{m=n+1}^\infty f(m) > \int_{n+1}^\infty f\).) Therefore the tail of eigenvalues is
\[
> \frac2{5} \cdot \frac1{8106} = \frac2{40530}.
\]
Finally, \(\frac2{40530} > \frac1{20260000}\) holds if and only if \(2 \cdot 20260000 > 40530\), or \(40520000 > 40530\), which is immediate. (Equivalently, \(\frac2{40530} \cdot 20260000 = \frac{40520000}{40530} > 999 > 1\).) This yields the strict inequality
\[
I < \frac12 - \frac1{10000 \times 2026},
\]
contradicting the earlier lower bound on \(I\). Thus the assumption is false: there must exist some \(k > 2026\) and some \(t \in [0,1]\) with \(| \int_0^t e_k(x)\,dx | > \frac1{100k}\).

(Note: the specific constants 2026 and 100 ensure the tail estimates are separated as above; the argument works for any sufficiently large fixed \(N\) and sufficiently small coefficient in place of \(1/100\).)
